title: Design a Collaborative Document Editor (Google Docs)
body:
# Design a Collaborative Document Editor (Google Docs)

> **Use case:** let many users edit the **same document concurrently in real time** — every keystroke
> from every editor appears on everyone's screen within a fraction of a second, and the document never
> diverges into conflicting copies.
> **Domain:** Google Docs, Notion, Figma, Microsoft Office for the web, code-pairing tools.
> **Scale:** hundreds of millions of documents; a single hot doc may have dozens of simultaneous
> editors; each editor emits **several edits per second** while typing.
> **Core challenges:** **conflict resolution** for concurrent edits (Operational Transformation vs
> CRDTs), **real-time sync** over WebSockets, **presence & cursors**, **persistence with periodic
> snapshots**, **offline edits & merge**, and **per-document fan-out**.

A text box looks trivial. The moment two people type into the same paragraph at the same instant, you
are forced to confront concurrency, causality, and conflict-free merging — the hardest problems in
distributed systems, on the critical path of every keystroke.

## 1 · Clarify requirements

**Functional**
- Multiple users edit one document; each sees others' edits **live** (target < 200 ms perceived lag).
- Edits **never silently overwrite** each other — concurrent inserts/deletes both survive in a sensible
  order.
- **Presence:** show who is in the doc, their **cursor/selection** position, live.
- **Persistence:** the document survives server restarts; a user reopening it gets the latest state.
- **Offline:** a user who loses connectivity can keep editing and have changes **merge** on reconnect.

**Non-functional**
- **Convergence:** all replicas that have seen the same set of edits show **identical** content
  (strong eventual consistency).
- **Low latency** on the keystroke path; **intent preservation** (your edit lands where you meant it,
  even after remote edits shifted the text).
- **Durability:** an acknowledged edit is never lost.
- **Per-doc fan-out** scales with editors; the system as a whole scales with millions of docs.

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "Why can't we just use last-write-wins on the whole document, the way a simple key-value store would?",
  "answer": "Last-write-wins (LWW) treats the document as one opaque value: whoever saves last replaces everyone else's version, so concurrent edits are destroyed wholesale — exactly the 'lost update' that collaborative editing must prevent. Even LWW at finer granularity fails because a text document has no stable keys: positions shift as text is inserted and deleted, so 'character at index 42' means different things to two editors who each typed before index 42. The correct model treats the document as a sequence of small operations (insert/delete at a position) and merges those operations so that BOTH survive and land where the author intended. That requires either transforming an operation against the concurrent operations it didn't see (Operational Transformation) or assigning every character a position-independent identity so order is deterministic regardless of arrival order (CRDT). Both guarantee convergence — every replica that has applied the same operation set shows identical text — which a coarse LWW value never can. So the requirement 'concurrent edits both survive, in the right place' rules out treating the doc as a single replaceable blob."
}
```

## 2 · Estimate the scale

```calc
{
  "title": "Op throughput for one busy document",
  "inputs": [
    { "key": "editors", "label": "Concurrent editors in the doc", "default": 20 },
    { "key": "opsPerSec", "label": "Edits/sec per active typist", "default": 5 },
    { "key": "activeFrac", "label": "Fraction actively typing", "default": 0.3 }
  ],
  "formula": "editors * opsPerSec * activeFrac",
  "resultLabel": "Inbound ops/sec for this doc",
  "resultUnit": "ops/s"
}
```

```calc
{
  "title": "Fan-out: messages the server pushes for one doc",
  "inputs": [
    { "key": "inboundOps", "label": "Inbound ops/sec (from calc above)", "default": 30 },
    { "key": "editors", "label": "Editors to broadcast to", "default": 20 }
  ],
  "formula": "inboundOps * (editors - 1)",
  "resultLabel": "Outbound messages/sec for this doc",
  "resultUnit": "msgs/s"
}
```

> One lively 20-person doc generates ~30 inbound ops/sec but ~**570 outbound messages/sec** — fan-out is
> the dominant cost. Multiply by millions of docs and the design becomes: **partition by document** so
> each doc's editors land on one server (cheap in-memory broadcast), and keep per-op payloads tiny
> (a single insert is a few dozen bytes). Storage is small per op, but logs grow forever → we **snapshot**.

## 3 · API & data model

The client speaks two things to its document server: it **streams ops** and **receives ops**. A thin
REST surface handles open/save; the live path is a persistent WebSocket.

```
POST /docs                         -> create, returns docId
GET  /docs/{id}                    -> latest snapshot + version (cold open)
WS   /docs/{id}/stream             -> bidirectional op channel:
        client -> server: { op, baseVersion, clientId }
        server -> client: { op, version }   (other editors' ops, transformed/ordered)
        server -> client: { presence: [{clientId, name, cursor, selection}] }
        client -> server: { presence: { cursor, selection } }
```

An **op** is the unit of change: `insert(pos, text)` / `delete(pos, len)` in the OT model, or
`insert(charId, afterId, value)` / `delete(charId)` in the CRDT model. Each op carries enough context
(a version vector or causal metadata) to merge correctly — this is **event sourcing**: the document is
the fold of its ordered operation log, and the stored snapshot is just a cached materialized view.

## 4 · High-level architecture

```flow
{
  "title": "Per-document collaboration pipeline",
  "nodes": [
    { "label": "Client editor", "detail": "Local model + optimistic apply; sends ops over WebSocket, renders remote ops & cursors instantly" },
    { "label": "Edge / LB (sticky by docId)", "detail": "Routes all editors of one doc to the SAME doc server so merge happens in one place" },
    { "label": "Doc server (owns the doc in memory)", "detail": "Sequences ops, transforms/merges, fans out to the doc's editors, tracks presence" },
    { "label": "Op log (append-only)", "detail": "Durable record of every op — event-sourced source of truth" },
    { "label": "Snapshot store", "detail": "Periodic materialized document + version, for fast cold opens & log truncation" },
    { "label": "Pub/sub (cross-server)", "detail": "Relays presence/ops if a doc's editors span more than one server during failover" }
  ],
  "note": "Routing all editors of a doc to one owner server turns fan-out into a cheap in-memory broadcast and gives a single serialization point for ordering."
}
```

**Storage choices.** The **op log** is append-only and write-heavy — a log-structured store (Kafka,
Bigtable, or an append table) fits. **Snapshots** go to a blob/document store keyed by `docId` plus the
version they cover. **Presence** is ephemeral and lives only in the doc server's memory (and pub/sub),
never persisted — it is throwaway state that vanishes when you disconnect.

The single most important architectural move: **one doc has one owner server at a time.** That gives a
single point to serialize concurrent ops into a total order, makes fan-out a local memory operation, and
keeps the merge logic simple. Stickiness is by `docId`, not user.

## 5 · Deep dive: conflict resolution — OT vs CRDT

Two users start from `"cat"`. Alice inserts `"s"` at end → `"cats"`. Bob, concurrently, inserts `"!"`
at end. Bob's op `insert(3,"!")` was written against length-3 text; after Alice's insert the text is
length 4, so naively applying Bob's op at index 3 puts `!` in the wrong place. **Both approaches fix
this — differently.**

```compare
{
  "options": [
    { "label": "Operational Transformation (OT)", "points": ["Ops carry positions (insert at index i)", "Server transforms each incoming op against concurrent ops it didn't see, shifting indices", "Needs a central server to serialize & transform — the classic Google Docs / Wave model", "Compact ops, small memory; transform functions are subtle and hard to get exactly right"] },
    { "label": "CRDT (sequence)", "points": ["Each character gets a unique, immutable, totally-ordered position id (e.g. fractional index / dense order)", "Merge is just union of ids; order is determined by the ids, not by arrival or transform", "Works peer-to-peer, no central transform needed — Figma, Yjs, Automerge", "Per-character metadata (ids, tombstones) → larger memory; needs garbage collection of deleted markers"] }
  ]
}
```

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "In the 'cat' example, how does each model make Alice's 's' and Bob's '!' both land correctly?",
  "answer": "OT (server-mediated): the server picks an order, say Alice first → text becomes 'cats'. Bob's op insert(3,'!') arrived based on the old length-3 text. The server TRANSFORMS Bob's op against Alice's already-applied insert(3,'s'): since Alice inserted at index 3 at-or-before Bob's position, Bob's index is shifted +1 to 4, yielding insert(4,'!') → 'cats!'. Every client applies the same transformed ops in the same server order and converges. The transform function (transform an insert against an insert, insert against delete, delete against delete, with tie-break rules for equal positions) is the whole ballgame, and getting every case right is famously error-prone. CRDT: there are no integer indices to shift. When Alice types 's' she mints a new character whose position id sorts after 't''s id; when Bob types '!' he mints an id that also sorts after 't' but, by tie-break on the unique site id embedded in each char id, sorts deterministically relative to Alice's 's'. Both characters are just added to the set; every replica sorts the set by id and gets the identical sequence 'cats!' (or 'cat!s' — whichever the id ordering dictates) regardless of who received which op first. No transformation and no central authority are required for correctness, only that ids are globally unique and densely orderable. The cost is that each char (including deleted ones, kept as tombstones so concurrent inserts next to them still order correctly) carries id metadata."
}
```

Drag the dial — central simplicity of merge logic vs decentralization and metadata cost:

```tradeoff
{
  "title": "OT vs CRDT for the editor's merge engine",
  "axis": { "left": "OT (central, compact ops)", "right": "CRDT (decentralized, heavier state)" },
  "steps": [
    { "label": "Pure OT", "detail": "Tiny index-based ops; a central server serializes and transforms everything. Smallest payloads, but a single ordering authority and notoriously tricky transform functions. Google Docs sits here." },
    { "label": "OT with op log replay", "detail": "OT plus an event-sourced log so late/offline clients catch up by replaying transformed ops from their last seen version. Still central, but recovers cleanly." },
    { "label": "CRDT with central relay", "detail": "Character-id CRDT, but a server still relays and persists for fan-out and durability. You get conflict-free merges without fragile transforms, paying per-char id overhead." },
    { "label": "Pure P2P CRDT", "detail": "Clients exchange ops directly, no server needed for correctness — works fully offline/peer-to-peer (Automerge/Yjs). Maximum resilience; largest metadata and GC burden." }
  ]
}
```

### Real-time sync & optimistic local apply

Latency hiding is essential: the editor **applies your op locally immediately** (optimistic), sends it,
and reconciles when the server's ordered version comes back. The client tracks a `baseVersion` so the
server (OT) knows which concurrent ops to transform against, or (CRDT) so it can ship only ops the client
hasn't seen.

```sequence
{
  "title": "Two concurrent edits, server-ordered and fanned out",
  "actors": ["Alice", "DocServer", "Bob"],
  "steps": [
    { "from": "Alice", "to": "Alice", "label": "type 's' → apply locally (optimistic), text='cats'" },
    { "from": "Bob", "to": "Bob", "label": "type '!' → apply locally, text='cat!'" },
    { "from": "Alice", "to": "DocServer", "label": "op insert(3,'s') @ baseVersion=v0" },
    { "from": "Bob", "to": "DocServer", "label": "op insert(3,'!') @ baseVersion=v0" },
    { "from": "DocServer", "to": "DocServer", "label": "serialize: Alice=v1, transform Bob → insert(4,'!')=v2; append to op log" },
    { "from": "DocServer", "to": "Bob", "label": "Alice's op v1 (transformed for Bob) → Bob converges to 'cats!'" },
    { "from": "DocServer", "to": "Alice", "label": "Bob's op v2 (transformed) → Alice converges to 'cats!'" }
  ]
}
```

### Persistence + periodic snapshots

The op log is the durable truth, but replaying millions of ops on every cold open is too slow. So the
doc server periodically writes a **snapshot** (the materialized document at version V) to the snapshot
store. Cold open = load latest snapshot + replay only the few ops after V. Old log entries before the
snapshot can be **compacted/truncated** (keeping enough history for offline clients and undo). This is
exactly the **event-sourcing + snapshot** pattern: log is authoritative, snapshot is a cache.

### Offline edits & merge

An offline client keeps editing against its last-known version, queuing ops locally. On reconnect it
sends its queue with the `baseVersion` it diverged from. **OT** transforms each queued op forward over
every op that landed while it was away; **CRDT** simply unions the queued char-ids into the set and
re-sorts — which is why CRDTs shine for offline-first and peer-to-peer. Either way, **causal metadata**
(version vectors / causal ordering) ensures an op is only applied after the ops it causally depends on,
preserving intent rather than scrambling the merge.

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "Why do presence and cursor positions get different treatment from document content?",
  "answer": "Content edits must be durable, causally ordered, and convergent — an acknowledged keystroke can never be lost, and all replicas must agree on the final text, so it goes through the op log, merge engine, and snapshots. Presence (who's online, where their cursor and selection are) is the opposite: it is ephemeral, high-frequency, and worthless a second later. If you lose a cursor update, the next one (arriving milliseconds behind, as the user keeps moving) corrects it; if a user disconnects, their presence should simply vanish. So presence is NOT written to the durable log or snapshots — it lives only in the doc server's memory and is broadcast best-effort, often throttled/coalesced (send at most ~10 cursor updates/sec rather than every pixel) to keep fan-out cheap. Cursor positions also need a subtlety: when remote text is inserted before your cursor, your stored cursor index must shift just like a character would, so cursors are transformed/anchored the same way ops are — but they are still treated as disposable overlay state, not part of the document's truth. Conflating the two (persisting every cursor wiggle through the op log) would multiply durable writes by an order of magnitude for data nobody ever needs to recover."
}
```

## 6 · Trade-offs & failure modes

- **Single owner per doc is a mini-SPOF.** If the owning doc server dies, that doc is briefly
  unavailable. Mitigate: the op log is durable, so a replacement server reloads the latest snapshot +
  tail of the log and re-accepts editors; clients reconnect (sticky re-route) and replay their pending
  ops. Recovery is fast because state is reconstructible from the log.
- **OT correctness risk.** Transform functions have many cases (insert/insert, insert/delete,
  delete/delete, equal-position tie-breaks); a single wrong case causes silent divergence. CRDTs move
  the risk into well-tested library code at the cost of metadata.
- **CRDT memory & tombstones.** Deleted characters can't be dropped immediately (a concurrent op may
  reference their position), so they linger as tombstones; long-lived docs need **garbage collection**
  once all replicas have seen the deletes.
- **Fan-out hot spots.** A doc that suddenly gets thousands of viewers (a viral shared doc) overwhelms
  one owner. Mitigate: separate **viewers** (read-only, can be fanned out via a tree of relay/edge
  servers or pub/sub) from the small set of **editors** that need the serialization point.
- **Causality vs latency.** Strict causal delivery can stall on a missing dependency; bound the wait and
  request a resync (snapshot) rather than blocking the editor indefinitely.

## 7 · Scaling & evolution

- **Partition by document.** Each doc independently owned; the fleet scales horizontally with doc count.
  A coordination service (or consistent-hash ring) assigns `docId → owner` and reassigns on failure.
- **Viewer fan-out tier.** Push read-only replicas down a fan-out tree / pub/sub so a popular doc's
  reads don't hit the editor-serialization path.
- **Snapshot cadence tuning.** Snapshot more often for hot docs (faster recovery, more storage), rarely
  for cold ones. Compact logs behind the oldest snapshot any offline client could still need.
- **Rich content beyond text.** Tables, comments, and embedded objects become additional CRDT/OT data
  types (maps, lists) composed into one document — the same convergence guarantees, more op types.
- **Beyond docs.** The exact pattern powers Figma (CRDT scene tree), code pairing, and multiplayer
  whiteboards; only the data type being merged changes.

## Self-test

```quiz
{
  "question": "Why route every editor of a single document to the same server?",
  "options": [
    "To reduce the user's bandwidth",
    "To get one serialization point for ordering/merging ops and make per-doc fan-out a cheap in-memory broadcast",
    "Because WebSockets only work on one server",
    "To avoid using a database"
  ],
  "answer": 1,
  "explanation": "One owner per doc gives a single place to totally-order and transform concurrent ops and lets the server broadcast to all editors from memory, instead of coordinating merges across machines."
}
```

```quiz
{
  "question": "What fundamentally distinguishes a CRDT sequence from Operational Transformation?",
  "options": [
    "CRDTs are faster on a single machine",
    "OT requires WebSockets; CRDTs use HTTP",
    "CRDTs give every character a unique, totally-ordered position id so merge is order-independent set union, needing no central transform; OT shifts integer indices and needs a server to serialize and transform ops",
    "CRDTs never need any server"
  ],
  "answer": 2,
  "explanation": "OT transforms position-based ops against concurrent ones (typically server-mediated). CRDTs encode order in immutable char ids, so merging is conflict-free union/sort regardless of arrival order — at the cost of per-char metadata and tombstones."
}
```

```quiz
{
  "question": "Why are periodic snapshots needed if the append-only op log is already the source of truth?",
  "options": [
    "Snapshots replace the log entirely",
    "Replaying the entire op log on every cold open is too slow; a snapshot lets you load state at version V and replay only the few ops after it (and truncate older log)",
    "Snapshots store presence data",
    "The log is not durable without them"
  ],
  "answer": 1,
  "explanation": "Event sourcing keeps the log authoritative, but a materialized snapshot is a cached view so opens are fast and old log entries can be compacted — presence is never persisted."
}
```

## Recap — key terms

Flip each card to check yourself, then move through the deck:

```flashcards
{
  "title": "Collaborative editor — key terms",
  "cards": [
    { "front": "Operational Transformation (OT)", "back": "Merge by transforming each incoming position-based op against the concurrent ops it didn't see, shifting indices. Central-server model (Google Docs); compact but tricky." },
    { "front": "CRDT (sequence)", "back": "Give every character a unique, densely-ordered id; merge = union + sort. Order-independent, no central transform, works offline/P2P. Costs per-char metadata + tombstones." },
    { "front": "Optimistic local apply", "back": "Apply your edit instantly on the client, send it, then reconcile with the server's ordered version — hides round-trip latency on the keystroke path." },
    { "front": "Op log + snapshot", "back": "Event-sourced append-only log is the truth; periodic materialized snapshots let cold opens load fast and let old log entries be compacted." },
    { "front": "Presence", "back": "Ephemeral who's-online + cursor/selection state. Broadcast best-effort, throttled, never persisted; vanishes on disconnect." },
    { "front": "Per-doc ownership / fan-out", "back": "Route all of one doc's editors to one server: single ordering point + cheap in-memory broadcast. Fan-out, not op count, dominates cost." }
  ]
}
```

## Key takeaways

- A collaborative editor is a **concurrency-and-convergence** problem: the document is an **ordered log
  of small ops**, not a replaceable blob, so concurrent edits both survive and land where intended.
- Choose the merge engine by **central simplicity vs decentralization**: **OT** (compact, server
  transforms index-based ops — Google Docs) vs **CRDT** (per-char ids, conflict-free union, offline/P2P
  friendly — Figma/Yjs) at the cost of metadata and tombstones.
- **Route every editor of a doc to one owner server** for a single ordering point and cheap fan-out;
  fan-out — not raw op throughput — is the dominant scaling cost.
- Persist via **event-sourced op log + periodic snapshots** (snapshot = cached view, log = truth);
  keep **presence ephemeral** and best-effort, never in the log.
- Handle **offline & merge** with causal metadata: OT replays/transforms queued ops forward, CRDTs
  union char-ids — both preserve causality so the merge keeps user intent.

## Concepts exercised

This design applies, end to end: `crdts` (the conflict-free sequence merge engine and its tombstones) ·
`causal-consistency` (version vectors / causal delivery so an op is applied only after its dependencies,
preserving intent across offline merges) · `realtime-communication` (the bidirectional WebSocket op
channel, optimistic local apply, and presence broadcast) · `event-sourcing` (the append-only op log as
source of truth with snapshots as cached materialized views). It also exercises `consistent-hashing`
(assigning `docId → owner` server), `single-point-of-failure` (the per-doc owner and its log-based
recovery), and `hot-partitions` (viral-doc viewer fan-out tiers).
